data.title: TTF Prices Down for 3rd Week
data.description:
European natural gas futures dropped over 7% this week to €33.8/MWh, marking a third straight week of declines and nearing seven-month lows.

The price slide is a relief for Europe, which faces a tough gas restocking season after a cold winter left storage unusually low.

Early injections have already started and are outpacing the five-year average, but challenges remain.

Markets found some relief after President Trump paused planned tariff hikes on most countries for 90 days, easing concerns about a broader global slowdown.

However, he raised tariffs on Chinese goods to 125%, keeping uncertainty high.

Traders remain cautious as it’s unclear how US-China trade tensions might affect LNG availability and flows to Europe.

data.title: Currencies firm as mighty dollar sinks; stocks subdued on tariff jitters
data.description:
Key points:
  • Currencies firm against a soft dollar
  • Singapore dollar at 5-month high
  • Asia EM equities find footing in afternoon trade
  • Singapore central bank to ease next week - Reuters poll
  • Xi Jinping to visit Southeast Asia next week

Asian currencies rose against the dollar on Friday as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on trading partners prompted a rush out of the world's reserve currency, though stocks remained subdued on lingering tariff concerns.

The U.S. dollar index fell below 100 against a basket of major units for the first time since July 2023 as investors ditched U.S. assets for other safe-havens like the Swiss franc, yen, euro and gold.

Singapore's dollar firmed to its highest level in more than five months, while Malaysia's ringgit appreciated by 0.7%. The Philippine peso and Vietnam's dong hit a one-week high, while the Indonesian rupiah was largely flat.

Taiwan's dollar hit an over six-week high, and South Korea's won edged up. An MSCI index of emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) touched its best level in a week.

"A broadly weaker dollar amid a trade slowdown makes the monetary policy picture more clear for EM central banks," said Alex Wolf, head of Asia Investment Strategy at J.P. Morgan Private Bank.

"A weaker USD driven by financial outflows as global investors soured on the U.S. exceptionalism has boosted EM currencies (with a few exceptions), providing central banks more leeway in setting policy."

On Thursday, the Philippine central bank resumed its easing cycle, cutting its key interest rate by a quarter-point. Next week, Singapore's central bank is widely expected to reduce the slope of its policy band by at least 50 basis points.

Traders resumed the sell-off in Asian emerging equities a day after a rally prompted by U.S. President Donald Trump's abrupt announcement of a 90-day pause in hefty import tariffs, though sentiment improved as the day progressed.

Trump's erratic tariff policies and their potential economic fallout have eroded appetite for risk, with the intensifying trade war with China, Southeast Asia's largest trade partner, keeping traders on edge.

Stocks in Singapore were last trading 1.7% lower after falling as much as 3%, while those in Malaysia and Thailand were down around 0.4% after falling up to 2%.

Indonesia's benchmark drifted within a tight range after an early 1.7% drop. Philippine stocks reversed course to trade largely flat.

Most Southeast Asian stock gauges were set to end the week deep in the red.

An MSCI index gauge of Asian emerging market equities (.MIMS00000PUS) reversed course from early losses, boosted by Taiwan's stocks TWSE:TAIEX, which jumped 2.8% after an initial 3% dip.

The East Asian country expects to be included in the first batch of trading partners to hold talks with Washington.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** FTSE ASEAN 40 index (.FTASEAN40) down 0.4%, led by Singapore banks

** China's Xi to visit Southeast Asia as trade conflict with U.S. deepens

** Indonesian companies allocate nearly $900 million for share buybacks, regulator says

** Facing Trump tariffs, Vietnam eyes crackdown on some China trade

data.title: Rheinmetall Expects Around Half of NATO Budgets to Fund EquipmentMarket Talk
data.description: Rheinmetall expects the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to increase its defense spending target to between 2.5% and 3.5% of gross domestic product at its June summit, and allocate around half of its budgets to military equipment, according to Morgan Stanley. The bank hosted the German arms maker's Chief Financial Officer Klaus Neumann for a roadshow in New York and Boston. Rheinmetall said it expects to win market share for order intake from 17% to 20-25% in the coming years, analysts Marie-Ange Riggio and Ross Law say. Second-quarter orders could be low due to the change of government in Germany, with new orders from higher defense spending likely coming in from late third-quarter or fourth-quarter, they add. Rheinmetall's 2025 guidance could still be updated, they say. Shares are up 1.1% at 1,395 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Hedge funds miss out on US equities rally but suffer smaller losses in volatile two days
data.description:
Global equities long/short hedge funds missed out on most of Wednesday's massive rally in U.S. stocks, triggered by President Donald Trump's pause on some tariffs for 90 days, but managed to limit their losses during Thursday's sell-off.

The funds were up 0.98% on April 9, while the S&P 500 index soared 9.5%, according to numbers compiled by Morgan Stanley sent to clients. U.S. hedge funds posted higher gains, up 2.28%, but still underperformed the index.

These funds turned negative on Thursday as the S&P 500 slumped 3.5% and the MSCI World (.MIWO00000PUS) index fell 1.2% on market jitters of more risks ahead.

Global fundamental long/short funds tracked by Goldman Sachs' prime brokerage desk were down 0.7% while systematic ones fared better, down 0.2%.

U.S.-focused funds led the losses on the day, returning -1.4%.

In a U-turn, Trump paused tariffs on Wednesday following a day when U.S. Treasuries sold off and showed signs of dislocations as investors feared the new trade policy could drive the world's largest economy into a recession.

Stocks rallied and Treasury yields pared back some of their gains after the announcement.

The rally caught hedge funds with increased short positions, or bets that stock prices would fall, by surprise.

Hedge funds underwent the largest selling on a net basis in almost 15 years a week ago, while also turning the most bearish since 2011, Goldman said.

REDUCING LEVERAGE

With a reduced portfolio of long positions - betting prices would go up -, hedge funds did not take much advantage of the rally, but also managed to avoid oversized losses in Thursday's plunge.

"Some of the activity (on Wednesday) was down to hedge funds covering their shorts after Trump's 90-day pause announcement," Jon Caplis, CEO of hedge fund research firm PivotalPath, said.

According to Goldman, hedge funds bought U.S. equities at the largest notional value in more than a decade on Wednesday, due to short covering in macro products.

Still, equity hedge funds are outperforming the S&P in 2025. Year-to-date through April 10, global fundamental long/short funds were down 4.3%, Goldman said. The S&P 500 dropped 10.4% in the same period.

Morgan Stanley's numbers also showed that managers continued to cut leverage in their portfolios. Amid uncertainty around tariffs, hedge fund managers have slashed positions to reduce risk.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0718 GMT - Rheinmetall expects the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to increase its defense spending target to between 2.5% and 3.5% of gross domestic product at its June summit, and allocate around half of its budgets to military equipment, according to Morgan Stanley. The bank hosted the German arms maker's Chief Financial Officer Klaus Neumann for a roadshow in New York and Boston. Rheinmetall said it expects to win market share for order intake from 17% to 20-25% in the coming years, analysts Marie-Ange Riggio and Ross Law say. Second-quarter orders could be low due to the change of government in Germany, with new orders from higher defense spending likely coming in from late third-quarter or fourth-quarter, they add. Rheinmetall's 2025 guidance could still be updated, they say. Shares are up 1.1% at 1,395 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0657 GMT - SATS faces an earnings hit from the tariff war, which poses substantial uncertainties for the global trade outlook in the medium term, UOB Kay Hian's Roy Chen says in a research report. SATS, because of its significant revenue exposure to the global and U.S. air cargo handling business, is poised to be hurt by a potential setback in globalization and decline in global trade volume, the analyst says. The brokerage cuts its air cargo volume projections for the Singapore-listed air cargo handler by 5% and lowers its earnings projections for SATS by 17%-18% for FY 2026-2027. It downgrades the stock's rating to hold from buy and lowers the target price to S$2.89 from S$4.00. Shares are 1.1% lower at S$2.62. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - Japanese stocks ended lower as growing U.S.-China trade tensions weighed on the global economic outlook. Electronics, pharmaceutical and auto shares led the losses. Sony Group dropped 7.4%, Chugai Pharmaceutical lost 6.4%, and Nissan Motor shed 6.3%. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 3.0% to 33585.58. Tariff-related headlines remain in focus. USD/JPY is at 144.13, down from 144.44 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. The 2-year Japanese government bond yield dropped 7 bps to 0.605%, the 10-year yield fell 2.5 bps to 1.345% while the 20-year yield rose 6 bps at 2.365%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0650 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening higher with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.9% at around 2301. The relief rally was short-lived and yesterday saw new steep stock market falls in the U.S., SEB analysts say in a note. "The pessimism stems from the fact that recession risks for the United States very much remain." There is still no tariff deal with any country that has received the 90-day tariff suspension while the conflict with China has worsened, SEB says. In Asia, stock markets are mixed. "It's obvious that the market is preparing for worse times. At the same time, not everything is pitch black." Ahead of the stock market opening today, futures are pointing up in both Europe and the U.S. OMXS30 closed at 2280.57, OMXN40 at 2119.65 and OBX at 1329.94. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0625 GMT - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 closed 0.8% down at 7646.5, paring early losses but still finishing lower across a rollercoaster week that featured its largest loss and largest gain since March 2020. The benchmark index dropped as much as 2.4% early Friday after U.S. equities lost more ground on worries over the impact of tariffs. It pared its losses through the day, helped by the consumer discretionary sector's 0.4% rise. The other 10 sectors finished in the red, with banks, REITs and health stocks all falling. Advances by gold miners partially offset more weakness in iron-ore and lithium stocks. The ASX 200 lost 0.3% for the week and remains in correction territory, down 11% off its mid-February record. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0609 GMT - Logitech International's decision to withdraw guidance for fiscal 2026 doesn't come as a surprise and seems sensible in light of uncertainty about trade tariffs, Vontobel's Michael Foeth says in a research note. The Swiss maker of computer peripherals generates around a third of its revenue from customers in the U.S., with 40% of its products made in China and the rest in Vietnam, Taiwan, Malaysia, Thailand and Mexico, according to Vontobel. "The high applicable tariffs on products from China and the uncertainty on further tariffs from other countries will inevitably have an impact on gross margins," the analyst says.(adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0520 GMT - New Zealand's NZX-50 closed 1.5% lower at 12019.13, rounding out its second-worst trading week of 2025 as tariffs continued to roil global markets. The benchmark index opened lower on Friday following a weak lead by U.S. equities and continued to drop through the morning before bouncing near session lows for the rest of the session. Aged-care providers, utilities, banks and logistics operators all closed in the red. Half of the eight largest losers were consumer-related, with Warehouse, Hallenstein Glasson, Tourism Holdings and KMD shedding between 3.6% and 5.9%. The NZX-50 lost 1.7% across the week and is down 9.4% on its most recent peak, reached in December. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Sweden's Hexagon says weak N.America, China growth hit Q1 earnings
data.description:
Swedish industrial technology group Hexagon said on Friday its adjusted operating profit fell around 8% in the first quarter due to weaker than expected growth in North America and China, sending its shares 6% lower.

In a preliminary statement, the maker of measurement and positioning systems said it expected quarterly adjusted earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) to fall to 345 million euros ($388.5 million), from 376.5 million last year.

"Growth in the key NAFTA and China markets declined in the last two weeks of March, with economic uncertainty impacting deliveries," Hexagon said in a statement.

The North American Free Trade Agreement, or NAFTA, concerns the United States, Canada and Mexico.

Hexagon also said its adjusted operating margin would decrease to about 26.1% in the first quarter, compared to 29% a year earlier.

($1 = 0.8881 euros)

data.title: M&C Saatchi's Nonexecutive Chair to Step Down in May
data.description:
M&C Saatchi's Nonexecutive Chair Zillah Byng-Thorne will step down after the company's annual general meeting scheduled for May 15.

The British advertising group said Friday that Dame Heather Rabbatts will assume the interim nonexecutive chair role.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0720 GMT - The probability of stagflation in the U.S. is more likely than recession due to inflation shocks from tariffs, says Blerina Uruci, Chief U.S. Economist at T. Rowe Price. The extent to which inflation shock leads to sustained inflation depends largely on how it influences consumer and business inflation expectations. A rapid succession of these shocks increases the probability of stagflation, she says. Attempts by the current administration to excessively curb immigration could also lead to tighter labor markets and sustained inflationary pressures, she says. Supply shocks from tariffs and potential fiscal boosts could further exacerbate the supply-demand mismatch and increase the likelihood of stagflation, she adds.(monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0709 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi fell 0.5% to close at 2432.72, dragged by auto and battery stocks. The index lost 1.3% for the week. Foreign investors were net sellers in Friday's session after declines on Wall Street overnight. The U.S.-China trade tensions remain elevated following a clarification by the White House that U.S. tariffs on China total 145% and not 125%. Carmakers Hyundai Motor and Kia slid 5.1% and 7.0%, respectively, as 25% U.S. levies on car imports continued to weigh on sentiment. USD/KRW settled 0.4% lower at 1,449.90 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was up 2.6 bps at 2.728%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - German Bunds and other eurozone government bond yields are little changed in early trading, outperforming U.S. Treasurys whose yields rise. "Earlier this week we noticed that the latest bout of risk-off has re-affirmed the international safe-haven status of German sovereign paper relative to U.S. Treasurys," Citi Research's Andrea Appeddu says in a note. The 10-year Bund yield is trading 0.4 basis point higher at 2.595%, while the 10-year Treasury yield is up 6.5 basis points, last trading at 4.458%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - Japanese stocks ended lower as growing U.S.-China trade tensions weighed on the global economic outlook. Electronics, pharmaceutical and auto shares led the losses. Sony Group dropped 7.4%, Chugai Pharmaceutical lost 6.4%, and Nissan Motor shed 6.3%. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 3.0% to 33585.58. Tariff-related headlines remain in focus. USD/JPY is at 144.13, down from 144.44 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. The 2-year Japanese government bond yield dropped 7 bps to 0.605%, the 10-year yield fell 2.5 bps to 1.345% while the 20-year yield rose 6 bps at 2.365%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0641 GMT - The dollar slides, with the DXY dollar index dropping to its lowest in 21 months against a basket of currencies overnight, after the White House said Thursday that tariffs imposed on China add up to 145%, not the 125% previously indicated. "While this difference is negligible in any practical economic sense, the market reaction showed an increased sensitivity to the risks of a disorderly economic decoupling between the world's two largest economies," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. This overshadows previous relief about a delay in additional tariffs on countries except China. The DXY dollar index is last down 0.3% at 100.530, having hit a low of 99.705 overnight. The euro rises 0.6% to $1.1261, having hit a three-year high of 1.1373 overnight, according to FactSet. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0606 GMT - The recent volatility in the U.S. Treasury market has renewed speculation about what is driving the sell-off, say rate strategists at Societe Generale Research in a note. They point to foreign divestment and the unwinding of leveraged basis trades as two key forces being increasingly in focus. "Either or both appear to have likely contributed to strained liquidity conditions and amplifying market moves," the strategists say. They expect realized volatility in global bonds to remain high during the 90-day tariff pause as markets digest the impact of the higher tariff regime and recalibrate to incoming headlines. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0558 GMT - The Monetary Authority of Singapore is likely to ease its policy settings at its meeting Monday, according to all 10 economists and analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal. Singapore Prime Minister Wong's view of a "profound turning point" in global affairs and downside risks strike a clearly dovish tone, Citi Research's Wei Zheng Kit says, noting Wong's Ministerial Statement on April 8. There's a 65% probability for a measured 50bp reduction in the slope of the MAS' SGD nominal effective exchange rate policy band, the economist says in a research report. Unlike most central banks, the MAS' monetary policy framework is centered on managing SGD against trade-weighed basket of currencies. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0550 GMT - The U.S. Treasury's auction of 30-year bonds on Thursday was strong, coming in rich versus the secondary market, ING rates strategists say in a note. "The indirect bid was strong (mostly foreign). The direct bid (more domestic) too," they say. Auction metrics were good, and "this is a vote of positivity on Treasuries," the strategists say. A perception of value likely helped as the 30-year yield rose in the hours into the auction, they say. "Still, a strong 30-year auction following a strong 10-year auction is quite a good look, especially in the wake of a really poor 3-year auction where central banks were expected to show up and lap up paper." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0547 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields are slightly higher in early European hours after sharp gyrations during the week on headlines around U.S. tariffs. "Our impression from here is the 10-year Treasury yield will have a tendency to head for the 4.5% area, potentially with a view to thinking about 4.75%," ING rates strategists say in a note. Directionally, there seems to be no mood to materially test lower in yield right now, despite the open door being presented by the risk-off backdrop, they say. The 10-year Treasury yield rises 5.5 basis points, last trading at 4.446%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0536 GMT - The Bank of Japan's latest consumer survey shows households continue to feel the pinch from rising prices, signaling that it's difficult for a virtuous cycle of income, spending and inflation to work smoothly in Japan. The quarterly survey shows nearly 56% say their living conditions are getting tougher, with more than 90% of them saying that's due to higher prices. Only 3.9% say their living conditions were easy. Policymakers hope wage growth will encourage more household spending, but the results suggest that consumer sentiment may not turn positive so quickly. Some 86.7% of the respondents say prices would increase further, either slightly or significantly, a year from now, according to the survey. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - Singapore's advance data is likely to show the economy grew 4.6% on year in 1Q, according to the median estimate of five economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal. That compares with a 5.0% growth in 4Q of 2024. 1Q GDP growth is likely to be robust, says SMBC's Jeff Ng says in an email. Policymakers have ruled out retaliation against the U.S.' 10% reciprocal tariff on imports from Singapore, but are reassessing downside risks to the economy, says the head of Asia Macro Strategy. The advance data is also tipped to show that GDP was flat on quarter on a seasonally adjusted basis, according to the poll. That would be down from 0.5% expansion in 4Q. The data are due Monday. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0524 GMT - The European Central Bank could cut interest rates more aggressively than currently priced in by money markets, although this might require a ​significant worsening in economic data, according to SEB Research. "We continue to predict that the ECB cuts the deposit facility rate below neutral, to 1.50% in September," it says. SEB Research's ECB view has received support from recent events around tariffs but continued back-to-back rate cuts need support from data in the coming months. Money markets currently price the terminal deposit rate, where the ECB stops rate cuts, at 1.75%, to be reached by October, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Chinese Chip Stocks Rise After Industry Group Issues Imports Guidance
data.description:
By Sherry Qin

Shares of Chinese chip makers rose after guidance from an industry association boosted expectations that tariffs on U.S. chips entering China could accelerate localization.

Chinese chip and chip-instrument makers jumped in China on Friday, as well as in Hong Kong, where they outperformed the Hang Seng Index's 1.2% gain. Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corp.--China's largest chip maker--rose 6.6% and 2.1% in Hong Kong and Shanghai, respectively.

China's No. 2 contract chip maker, Hua Hong Semiconductor, soared 15% in Hong Kong. Innoscience (Suzhou) Technology climbed 10% and Montage Technology added 7.1%.

That comes after China's Semiconductor Industry Association issued a notice with suggestions on declaring the origin of semiconductor imports after studying Chinese Customs documents. Some analysts say the move could shut a backdoor to bypass tariffs set by Beijing.

"Regardless of whether the 'integrated circuit' is packaged or unpackaged, the origin at the time of import declaration shall be declared as the location of the 'wafer fabrication factory,'" the association said.

The design, fabrication, packing and testing of semiconductors often takes place in different countries, making it hard for officials to identify the country of origin.

The notice came after China set a 84% tariff on U.S. products in response to President Trump's successive raising of tariffs on Chinese goods, which now stand at 145%.

Analysts say the industry group's suggestion on the origin of imports could give Chinese firms more of an edge in gaining domestic market share over U.S. rivals in mature chips and other chip-equipment and material.

Texas Instruments and Intel, who have substantial sales in China, make most of their China-bound chips in the U.S. before packaging them in Taiwan and other Southeast Asian countries.

The industry group clarified that doing so would mean the country of origin can be defined as the U.S., at the point of fabrication, rather than where the products were packaged. That would leave imports subject to higher tariff rates.

Bernstein analyst Qingyuan Liu said the clarification could see local companies get a bigger share of the market.

Domestic front-end wafer manufacturers and backend integrated circuit-packaging and testing vendors stand to benefit, Daiwa analyst Rick Hsu said, as do chip-equipment makers and suppliers of key materials such as raw wafers and chemicals.

Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com

data.title: BP Expects First-Quarter Upstream Production Decline as Debt RisesUpdate
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

BP said it expects to report lower first-quarter upstream production due to previously announced divestments in Egypt and Trinidad, while its debt jumped.

The British energy giant is in the process of a strategic reset and is pivoting back to oil from low-carbon energy amid pressure from investors while targeting net debt reductions via divestments.

In its first-quarter update, BP said production in its gas and low-carbon energy segments declined from the previous quarter, hurting total upstream production. The company divested of assets in Egypt and Trinidad at the end of last year, resulting in a base decline of around 90,000 barrels of oil equivalent a day, it said.

Net debt rose by $4 billion in the quarter, mainly due to seasonal inventory effects, annual bonus awards and payments related to low-carbon assets held for sale, it said. BP said it expects the increase to largely reverse. Its net debt stood at $23 billion at the end of last year.

Oil production is expected to have been slightly higher in the quarter but divisional earnings are expected to be broadly flat, partially due to price lags on production in the Gulf of Mexico and the United Arab Emirates, it said.

Stronger refining margins will provide a boost of $100 million to $300 million to its first-quarter earnings despite remaining low, BP said. For the quarter, its refining margin averaged $15.2 a barrel compared to $13.1 a barrel in the fourth-quarter 2024.

Last week, as part of its strategic shakeup, the oil giant said Chairman Helge Lund would stand down and likely leave the company next year.

Lund had championed BP's now jettisoned strategy which saw it expand into low-carbon energy such as wind, solar and electric-vehicle charging. The company's shift back to fossil fuels comes under pressure from investors over a lagging share price rises. Activist hedge fund Elliott Investment Management has taken a stake in the company with the intention of pushing for significant changes.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Spain's Annual Inflation Rate Declines to 2.3% in March, Final Data Shows
data.description:
As expected, Spain's annual inflation rate fell to 2.3% in March from 3% in the previous month, final data from the National Statistics Institute showed Friday.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices rose 0.1%, matching the initial reading, against the 0.4% increase earlier.

The Spanish annual harmonized inflation rate stood at 2.2%, in line with expectations. Month over month, harmonized consumer prices gained 0.7%, as expected, compared with the 0.4% rise in February.

Meanwhile, the country's annual core inflation rate edged down to 2% from 2.2%.

data.title: Currencies firm as mighty dollar sinks; stocks subdued on tariff jitters
data.description:
Key points:
  • Currencies firm against a soft dollar
  • Singapore dollar at 5-month high
  • Asia EM equities find footing in afternoon trade
  • Singapore central bank to ease next week - Reuters poll
  • Xi Jinping to visit Southeast Asia next week

Asian currencies rose against the dollar on Friday as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on trading partners prompted a rush out of the world's reserve currency, though stocks remained subdued on lingering tariff concerns.

The U.S. dollar index fell below 100 against a basket of major units for the first time since July 2023 as investors ditched U.S. assets for other safe-havens like the Swiss franc, yen, euro and gold.

Singapore's dollar firmed to its highest level in more than five months, while Malaysia's ringgit appreciated by 0.7%. The Philippine peso and Vietnam's dong hit a one-week high, while the Indonesian rupiah was largely flat.

Taiwan's dollar hit an over six-week high, and South Korea's won edged up. An MSCI index of emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) touched its best level in a week.

"A broadly weaker dollar amid a trade slowdown makes the monetary policy picture more clear for EM central banks," said Alex Wolf, head of Asia Investment Strategy at J.P. Morgan Private Bank.

"A weaker USD driven by financial outflows as global investors soured on the U.S. exceptionalism has boosted EM currencies (with a few exceptions), providing central banks more leeway in setting policy."

On Thursday, the Philippine central bank resumed its easing cycle, cutting its key interest rate by a quarter-point. Next week, Singapore's central bank is widely expected to reduce the slope of its policy band by at least 50 basis points.

Traders resumed the sell-off in Asian emerging equities a day after a rally prompted by U.S. President Donald Trump's abrupt announcement of a 90-day pause in hefty import tariffs, though sentiment improved as the day progressed.

Trump's erratic tariff policies and their potential economic fallout have eroded appetite for risk, with the intensifying trade war with China, Southeast Asia's largest trade partner, keeping traders on edge.

Stocks in Singapore were last trading 1.7% lower after falling as much as 3%, while those in Malaysia and Thailand were down around 0.4% after falling up to 2%.

Indonesia's benchmark drifted within a tight range after an early 1.7% drop. Philippine stocks reversed course to trade largely flat.

Most Southeast Asian stock gauges were set to end the week deep in the red.

An MSCI index gauge of Asian emerging market equities (.MIMS00000PUS) reversed course from early losses, boosted by Taiwan's stocks TWSE:TAIEX, which jumped 2.8% after an initial 3% dip.

The East Asian country expects to be included in the first batch of trading partners to hold talks with Washington.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** FTSE ASEAN 40 index (.FTASEAN40) down 0.4%, led by Singapore banks

** China's Xi to visit Southeast Asia as trade conflict with U.S. deepens

** Indonesian companies allocate nearly $900 million for share buybacks, regulator says

** Facing Trump tariffs, Vietnam eyes crackdown on some China trade

data.title: Strides Pharma Science Forms Step-Down Subsidiary; Shares Up 4%
data.description:
Strides Pharma Science has incorporated a step-down subsidiary called 'Pivot Path', a life sciences and manufacturing services provider, according to a Friday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The new unit is wholly owned by Arco Lab, which in turn is a wholly owned subsidiary of the company.

The new entity will engage in outsourcing and business support services for all processes, sub-processes, transactions, activities and all other work performed in various industries with a focus on the pharmaceutical sector, within India and overseas.

Shares of the company were up nearly 4% in recent trade.

data.title: Hopson Development's March Sales Hit 1.17 Billion Yuan
data.description:
Hopson Development's gross contracted sales reached 1.17 billion yuan in March, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The contract sales area for the month hit a gross floor area of 10,478 square meters, the filing said.

data.title: South Korea says two workers feared trapped in rail construction site collapse
data.description:
A rail construction site in South Korea has collapsed, with two workers feared trapped at the site in Gwangmyeong, southwest of the capital Seoul, the National Fire Agency said on Friday.

Cracks had been found in an underground pillar and reported to authorities overnight, according to media reports.

data.title: Logitech shares slip after it pulls 2026 guidance
data.description:
** Shares in Logitech slip around 1% after the computer parts maker on Thursday withdrew its forecast for 2026, citing the continuing uncertainty around tariffs

** The Swiss-American company previously forecast FY sales to range from $4.53 billion to $4.71 billion for 2026

** Vontobel says Logitech generates around 1/3 of its revenues from customers in the U.S. and 40% of the products shipped to the U.S. were so far produced in China and the rest in Vietnam, Taiwan, Malaysia, Thailand and Mexico

** "The high applicable tariffs on products from China and the uncertainty on further tariffs from other countries will inevitably have an impact on gross margins," it says

** The company nevertheless reaffirmed its FY outlook for 2025

** The stock, which has lost 23% YTD up to Thursday's close, is among the worst performers of Switzerland's blue-chip index

data.title: Slovenia's KD proposing 50 euro dividend/share
data.description:
LJUBLJANA (Slovenia), April 11 (SeeNews) - Slovenian holding company KD d.d. [LJE:SKDR] has proposed to pay out a gross dividend of 50 euro ($56.3) per share out of its distributable profit for 2024.

Overall, up to 7.8 million euro will be distributed as dividend from the company’s distributable profit of 37.9 million euro for last year and the remainder will be retained, KD said in a note to the Ljubljana Stock Exchange last week.

Record date is May 22, ex-dividend date is May 21 and payment date is May 23.

The shareholders will vote on the proposal on May 15.

Last year, KD distributed a dividend of 39.79 euro per share.

KD’s shares last traded on February 12, ending 4.95% lower at 480 euro.

($ = 0.888 euro)
